quiz_questions.question_text: On a position-time graph, what does a horizontal line mean?

options.text: The object has constant velocity
The object is accelerating
The object is at rest
The object is decelerating

quiz_questions.explanation: A horizontal line on a position-time graph means position is not changing, so the object is stationary (at rest). The slope is zero, meaning velocity = 0.

quiz_questions.question_text: On a v-t graph, what does a horizontal line mean?

options.text: Constant acceleration
Constant velocity (zero acceleration)
The object is decelerating
The object is at rest

quiz_questions.explanation: A horizontal line on a velocity-time graph means velocity is constant (not changing). The slope is zero, so acceleration = 0. The object moves at a steady speed in a fixed direction.

quiz_questions.question_text: What shape does constant acceleration produce on a position-time graph?

options.text: A straight line
A parabola
A horizontal line
A circle

quiz_questions.explanation: With constant acceleration, position depends on t^2 (from x = x0 + v0*t + 0.5*a*t^2), which produces a parabolic curve on a position-time graph.

quiz_questions.question_text: How do you go from an x-t graph to a v-t graph?

options.text: Take the slope (differentiate)
Add a constant
Take the area (integrate)
Multiply by time

quiz_questions.explanation: The slope of a position-time graph gives velocity. Going from x-t to v-t means taking the slope (derivative). Going the other direction (v-t to x-t) requires taking the area (integral).

quiz_questions.question_text: On an a-t graph, what does the area under the curve represent?

options.text: Displacement
Change in velocity
Acceleration
Force

quiz_questions.explanation: The area under an acceleration-time graph equals the change in velocity. Just as the area under a v-t graph gives displacement, the area under an a-t graph gives the change in velocity.

quiz_questions.question_text: On a position-time graph, what does a curved line mean?

options.text: Constant velocity
The object is moving backward
The object is at rest
Changing velocity (acceleration)

quiz_questions.explanation: A curved line on a position-time graph means the slope (velocity) is changing, which indicates the object is accelerating. A curve that gets steeper means the object is speeding up.

quiz_questions.question_text: How do you go from a v-t graph to an x-t graph?

options.text: Take the slope
Square the values
Take the area (integrate)
Divide by time

quiz_questions.explanation: The area under a velocity-time graph gives displacement (position change). So to go from v-t to x-t, you calculate the area under the curve (integrate).

quiz_questions.question_text: On a position-time graph, a straight line with a positive slope means the object is:

options.text: At rest with no motion at all
Moving at constant velocity in the positive direction
Accelerating in the positive direction
Moving at constant velocity in the negative direction

quiz_questions.explanation: A straight line on a position-time graph means constant velocity (no acceleration). A positive slope means the object moves in the positive direction.

quiz_questions.question_text: On a v-t graph, what does a straight line with a positive slope represent?

options.text: Constant positive acceleration
Constant negative acceleration
Zero acceleration (constant velocity)
Decreasing acceleration over time

quiz_questions.explanation: A straight line with positive slope on a v-t graph means velocity is increasing at a constant rate, which is constant positive acceleration.

quiz_questions.question_text: On a v-t graph, a line that slopes downward means the object is:

options.text: Speeding up in the positive direction
Decelerating (negative acceleration)
Moving at constant velocity forward
Completely at rest and not moving

quiz_questions.explanation: A downward slope on a v-t graph means velocity is decreasing, which indicates negative acceleration (deceleration). The object is slowing down.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the 'slope' of a graph represent in physics?

options.text: The rate of change of the y-axis quantity with respect to the x-axis quantity
The total area enclosed between the curve and the horizontal axis
The maximum value of the quantity shown on the vertical axis
The point where the graphed line crosses the horizontal x-axis

quiz_questions.explanation: The slope of a graph is rise over run, representing the rate of change of the y-axis variable with respect to the x-axis variable. On a position-time graph, slope = velocity.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'area under the curve' mean on a velocity-time graph?

options.text: The total displacement of the object during that time interval
The total acceleration of the object during that time interval
The average velocity of the object during that time interval
The total force applied to the object during that time interval

quiz_questions.explanation: The area under a velocity-time graph represents displacement. Positive area (above the time axis) is positive displacement; negative area (below) is negative displacement.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'tangent line' mean on a curved position-time graph?

options.text: A straight line that touches the curve at exactly one point and gives the instantaneous velocity
A horizontal line drawn at the highest point of the curve on the graph
A vertical line drawn from any point on the curve down to the time axis
A diagonal line connecting the first data point to the last data point on the graph

quiz_questions.explanation: A tangent line touches a curve at exactly one point. Its slope at that point gives the instantaneous velocity on a position-time graph (or instantaneous acceleration on a v-t graph).

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'intercept' mean on a position-time graph?

options.text: The initial position of the object when time equals zero
The point where the object reaches its maximum velocity
The total distance the object has traveled during the motion
The point where the object's acceleration changes direction

quiz_questions.explanation: The y-intercept on a position-time graph represents the object's position at time t = 0. It tells you where the object started.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the meaning of 'negative slope' on a position-time graph?

options.text: The object is moving in the negative direction (e.g., backward or left)
The object is accelerating in the positive direction over time
The object is completely at rest and not moving at all currently
The graph was drawn incorrectly and should be redone from scratch

quiz_questions.explanation: A negative slope on a position-time graph means the position is decreasing over time, so the object has a negative velocity (moving in the negative direction).

quiz_questions.question_text: What does it mean when two lines cross on a position-time graph?

options.text: The two objects are at the same position at that moment in time
The two objects have the same velocity at that moment in time
The two objects collide and stop moving at that moment in time
The two objects have the same acceleration at that moment in time

quiz_questions.explanation: When two lines cross on a position-time graph, the objects are at the same position at that time. They are not necessarily moving at the same velocity or colliding.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'steeper slope' mean on a position-time graph?

options.text: The object has a greater acceleration at that time
The object is moving faster (higher velocity) at that time
The object is at a higher position on the vertical axis
The object has a greater mass than the other objects plotted

quiz_questions.explanation: On a position-time graph, a steeper slope means a greater rate of position change, which corresponds to a higher velocity. The steeper the line, the faster the object moves.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does a 'concave up' curve on a position-time graph indicate?

options.text: The object is slowing down as time increases
The object is speeding up (positive acceleration)
The object has constant velocity throughout its motion
The object is moving in the negative direction continuously

quiz_questions.explanation: A concave up (bowl-shaped) curve on a position-time graph means the slope is increasing, indicating the velocity is getting larger over time, which means positive acceleration.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the term 'integrate' mean in the context of motion graphs?

options.text: To find the area under a graph curve to determine the next quantity
To combine two separate graphs into one single graph on the same axes
To draw a best-fit line through scattered data points on the graph
To extend the graph line beyond the measured data to predict values

quiz_questions.explanation: In motion graphs, integrating means finding the area under a curve. The area under a v-t graph gives displacement, and the area under an a-t graph gives change in velocity.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'differentiate' mean in the context of motion graphs?

options.text: To find the slope of a graph curve to determine the next quantity
To separate a single graph into two different graphs for comparison
To identify the differences between two objects on the same graph
To convert a curved line into a straight line for easier analysis

quiz_questions.explanation: In motion graphs, differentiating means finding the slope. The slope of an x-t graph gives velocity, and the slope of a v-t graph gives acceleration.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student looks at a v-t graph that shows a straight line from (0, 0) to (4, 20). What is the acceleration?
quiz_questions.explanation: Acceleration = slope = rise/run = (20 - 0)/(4 - 0) = 20/4 = 5 m/s^2. The object speeds up from 0 to 20 m/s over 4 seconds at a constant rate.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student has a v-t graph showing constant velocity of 10 m/s for 5 seconds. What is the displacement?

options.text: 2 m

quiz_questions.explanation: Displacement = area under the v-t graph = base x height = 5 s x 10 m/s = 50 m. The area forms a rectangle with base 5 and height 10.

quiz_questions.question_text: A lab group plots position vs. time for a toy car and gets a straight line with a negative slope. What can they conclude?

options.text: The car is accelerating in the forward direction
The car is moving at constant velocity in the negative direction
The car is at rest and not moving at all
The car is decelerating and coming to a stop

quiz_questions.explanation: A straight line means constant velocity (no acceleration). A negative slope means the velocity is negative (moving in the negative direction). The car moves backward at a constant rate.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student sees a v-t graph that shows velocity increasing from 0 to 15 m/s linearly over 3 seconds. What is the total displacement during this time?

options.text: 5 m

quiz_questions.explanation: The area under the line is a triangle: area = 0.5 x base x height = 0.5 x 3 x 15 = 22.5 m. The displacement equals the area under the v-t graph.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student analyzes an x-t graph and sees a parabola curving upward. On the corresponding v-t graph, what shape should they expect?

options.text: A horizontal line at zero velocity
A straight line with positive slope
A parabola curving upward identically
A straight line with negative slope

quiz_questions.explanation: An upward parabola on an x-t graph indicates constant positive acceleration. The velocity increases linearly with time, so the v-t graph is a straight line with positive slope.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a lab, a student's v-t graph shows the velocity going from 10 m/s to -10 m/s over 4 seconds in a straight line. What is the acceleration?
quiz_questions.explanation: Acceleration = slope = (-10 - 10)/4 = -20/4 = -5 m/s^2. The object decelerates, stops, and reverses direction during the 4-second interval.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student looks at two position-time graphs. Object A has a steeper straight line than Object B. Which object is moving faster?

options.text: Object B, because a less steep line shows higher velocity
Object A, because a steeper slope means higher velocity
They move at the same speed because both lines are straight
Neither, because slope does not relate to velocity at all

quiz_questions.explanation: On a position-time graph, slope = velocity. A steeper slope means a larger velocity. Object A has a steeper line, so it moves faster than Object B.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student plots an a-t graph that shows a constant acceleration of 2 m/s^2 for 5 seconds. Starting from rest, what is the change in velocity?

options.text: 10 m/s
7 m/s
25 m/s

quiz_questions.explanation: Area under a-t graph = change in velocity = base x height = 5 s x 2 m/s^2 = 10 m/s. Starting from rest, the final velocity is 10 m/s.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student's x-t graph shows a curve that starts steep and becomes flatter over time. What does this tell you about the object's motion?

options.text: The object is speeding up as time goes on
The object is slowing down as time goes on
The object maintains constant velocity throughout
The object is stationary and not moving at all

quiz_questions.explanation: A curve that starts steep and becomes flatter means the slope (velocity) is decreasing over time. The object is slowing down (decelerating).

quiz_questions.question_text: A student has a v-t graph showing 4 m/s for the first 3 seconds, then 8 m/s for the next 2 seconds. What is the total displacement?
quiz_questions.explanation: Displacement = area under the v-t graph. First rectangle: 4 x 3 = 12 m. Second rectangle: 8 x 2 = 16 m. Total: 12 + 16 = 28 m.
